text:
🎆浴衣デート　どっきりする1シーンガチャ🎆

諸君、夏祭りの青春の1ページを生成画像で掴もうではないか…

ガチャ配当として
振り向き：2割
屋台で楽しそうにこっちを見る：2割
正面から少し覗き込む：2割
カメラにピース：2割
かき氷を「あーん」してくる：1割
袖を持ってウインク：1割

是非ひと夏の思い出を生成してみてください
#AIart #CharGPT #生成画像AI #Prompt
------------以下プロンプト----------
Ultra-realistic vertical 9:16 iPhone snapshot.

A clearly adult Japanese woman in her mid-to-late 20s during a summer festival date in Japan.

Every generation should randomly choose one of the following candid festival-date moments:

20%:
She has just turned around after hearing her name called.
A natural smile begins to appear, and her eyes briefly meet the camera.

20%:
She is standing near a food stall, laughing happily while looking toward the camera, holding festival food or a small drink naturally.

20%:
She faces the camera from the front and leans slightly closer, as if playfully peeking into the camera during the date.

20%:
She looks at the camera and makes a casual peace sign with a bright natural smile, like a real iPhone photo taken by someone close to her.

10%:
She holds a cup of shaved ice and playfully offers a spoonful toward the camera, creating a sweet “aah” moment without looking staged.

10%:
She gently holds the sleeve of her yukata and gives a small playful wink, natural and cute rather than model-like.

She has a soft oval face, a small delicate jawline, large dark-brown eyes with gentle warmth, smooth healthy skin, soft pink lips, and long dark-brown hair with natural wispy bangs.

Her beauty feels like a perfect balance between:
a clean and elegant gyaru with natural charm,
an approachable idol-level beauty,
a kind young wife with a warm personality,
and the girl-next-door everyone secretly remembers.

She is beautiful but not intimidating.

She looks like someone who could realistically exist in everyday life, yet somehow impossible to forget.

She is wearing a beautiful modern yukata with a realistic fabric texture, soft seasonal colors, natural folds, and tasteful festival styling.

No fashion pose.
No model expression.
No professional photoshoot.
No studio lighting.
No overly perfect composition.

Natural smartphone photography.

Warm lantern lights.

Authentic Japanese summer festival atmosphere.

Food stalls, paper lanterns, distant laughter, festival games, goldfish scooping, soft crowds, and warm summer night lighting fill the background.

Slight motion blur.

Imperfect framing.

Real smartphone flash mixed with ambient lantern lighting.

Subtle sensor noise.

Natural compression artifacts.

The viewer feels as if they are standing beside her during a real summer festival date.

The moment feels nostalgic, memorable, and emotionally powerful.

The beauty comes from realism, warmth, and emotional connection rather than glamour.

Mood:
summer memories,
nostalgia,
gentle attraction,
warmth,
romance,
friendship,
a fleeting moment that feels impossible to recreate.

The image should make the viewer feel:

"I wish I had experienced this moment."

Tomorrow. Free. 

@EricBDelisle has gathered a bunch of people for a virtual conference trying to answer how humans will deal as we go into the singularity. 

I've been talking to many who are building the AI future, studying them, and talking with many others on audio spaces here on X. 

I'm working on my speech today, which will be about 15 minutes. 

Might be bombastic: "your humanoid will find your sex toys and guns within a few minutes of arriving in your home." 

OK, it'll take a little longer than that, but not much. :-)

But mostly I'll be talking about all the poor parenting decisions I made parenting my kids, starting with giving them an iPad when they were a year and a half old. Now are 16 and 18. 

I don't yet see glimmers of the next big tech titan in them, but that's OK. 

Having a special needs kid meant giving up visions of having a super star kid. 

He's turned into a super star in my own eyes, as I've watched him deal with challenges most of us haven't had to deal with. 

And motivates me to find my own lane through this world and hopefully inspire them to find theirs. 

Their lives are gonna be far more weird than ours were. 

When I was their age we didn't have autonomous vehicles, lights with computers, AIs that we could argue about whether or not they are conscious, or satellites that spray the internet down to a white antenna. Among many other things.

They are gonna see robots become ubiquitous, brain computer interfaces that will let their minds perceive reality in ways that we can hardly imagine now, and AIs that will run literally everything in their lives (one already makes toast for them in the morning). 

So much change has already hit, and we are in an exponential world, which means every year will see even more change than the year before. And not stop the rest of their lives.

Which is why I start with a humanoid robot cleaning a home in a decade. It freaks people out, gets their attention, but we need to prepare our minds for it.

Next week I'll be speaking about the same at @JoinEdgeCity and trying to do the same.

And I'll be doing the talk from San Francisco. A place that always makes me feel behind.

Which is a new problem: how do you deal with a world where you are always behind? Especially if you are like me and used to always being ahead (I wrote a book about social media a year before this Website started, for instance). 

On the way to San Francisco tonight I'll be talking to Grok as our Tesla drives about all these issues. 

What are you doing to deal with the change that keeps hitting harder and harder? 

Meditation? Walks in a forest? 

Or are you like one entrepreneur who told me last week: "just do things that are fun and you'll never burn out?"

Many don't have that privilege, I know. I visited slums around the world to see how difficult billions of people on earth have it compared to me. 

Will try to bring that learning into the 15 minutes I've been gifted too.

🔌Today I created a virtual web-based null modem so now you can finally play Quake 1 (from 1996) in multiplayer in the browser with other people online!

This week I was able to create a virtual printer that listens on the COM2 port to make a web-based printer work in Windows 3.11, like on you can actually print to, it was one of my ideas when I started https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC but it was always too difficult to make, I had no idea where to start, but this week AI was able to do it!

Today I woke up and thought "if we can listen to COM2 via JS, how about listen to COM1 from Quake 1?" then we could forward the COM1 data via Websockets and to another user to create a virtual null modem

(A null modem was a cable you'd use to connect two computers to each other directly in the 90s, if you couldn't afford buying a network card you'd use this to network, but you could only play with one other computer not multiple)

So I built it, it took about an hour and works! I wanted to get Quake 1 in DOS to work with multiplayer for 2 years but I could never get it to work, but now it did it

This project is a good benchmark of how capable AI is becoming, figuring stuff out that humans would take months within an hour

You can try it here, I don't know how it will match lots of people but let's see, you have to exit to DOS first (ALT-F4 or FN-Option-F4 on Mac), then type CD games, then CD quake, then quake, then ESC, then Multiplayer, then either Create New Game or Join Game, and use Direct Connect, keep settings same and you're in!

https://t.co/iMd5mzaPQX

Can one diffusion model master multiple text-to-image tasks without forgetting?

Researchers from Fudan University & Alibaba Group present DiffusionOPD. 

Instead of joint training, they first train separate expert teachers, then distill their knowledge into a single student along its own rollout. This decouples learning and avoids cross-task interference.

Result: Outperforms both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines, achieving state-of-the-art on all benchmarks.

DiffusionOPD: A Unified Perspective of On-Policy Distillation in Diffusion Models 

Paper: https://t.co/j0ehhYqLOC
Project: https://t.co/JSUN5xMRUR
Code: https://t.co/yOJC1Ndm32

Our report: https://t.co/WjqtQhGfD0

📬 #PapersAccepted by Jiqizhixin

Training diffusion & flow policies with RL is hard, but training them with behavioral cloning is easy. So what if we just train flow policies with BC, and only "do RL" at test time?

We found an easy way to do this (called Q-Guided Flow), and some surprising findings👇🧵 https://t.co/lUpW8iQWDB

(To be clear, *I* don't think we should try to slow down recursive AI self improvement - I think we should open it up and democratize it as much as possible. My point is: if *you* claim we should slow down, and you have the best model, you should ensure your org can't use it.)

Diffusion is differentiable. LLMs aren't.

So why is the diffusion community copying RL methods (GRPO etc.) from LLMs?

The native post-training for diffusion is gradient descent such as ReFL and LeapAlign. Paper: https://t.co/uoy9mCGJSv
